title: 23 Hours, $92, 44 Subagents: Building an AI Family Calendar with OPC Loop
summary: One session from Google Calendar clone redesign to AI Agent Feed to LLM Pool three-way fallback—all driven by OPC loop. Not a demo, but real product iteration including pivots, gotchas, and agents reviewing each other's work.
body:
I first realized this session was different when I saw the screenshot of the family calendar main interface.

It wasn't a Google Calendar clone. It was an AI agent feed—"Good evening, xingjingx 👋" at the top, empty bento stats cards below, and a FabBar at the bottom saying "Tell me something new..." Less than 24 hours after we wrote the first line of code.

This article is a postmortem of that session. Not a "what we built" technical doc, but "what's worth remembering from this process"—including why we pivoted mid-stream, how the LLM Pool was designed, and what OPC loop can actually accomplish in 23 hours.

---

## From Calendar to Agent Feed

The project started with a user's request:

> "The main interface should be more human friendly—easy to add new things, easy to see previous ones, mostly agent reminders, rebuild it"

The previous session had already run through 21 OPC ticks, taking a family calendar app from scaffold to a "complete" version with Auth, DB migrations, LLM parsing, and Web Push notifications. That version's main interface was a Google Calendar replica: month view, week view, click cells to add events.

But that was wrong.

The core problem with family calendars isn't "where events live," but "why would family members open this every day?" A calendar you have to actively check isn't much different from a paper wall calendar. The real value is agents proactively helping you remember, remind you, notify you cross-channel. Calendar view is a tool you use when needed, not the first screen you open daily.

So we pivoted: **main interface becomes agent feed**. Calendar gets demoted to a secondary `/calendar` page. First screen shows today's schedule, agent suggestion cards, FabBar for casual event input.

This redesign used OPC loop + subagent-driven-development, 13 tasks running parallel/serial, resulting in 35 git commits worth of code. Total process: $92 spend, 1389 minutes, 44 subagents.

---

## The Real Engineering Highlight: LLM Pool

Main interface redesign was a product decision; LLM Pool was an engineering decision. The latter is more worth discussing.

It started when the user asked while reviewing agent functionality:

> "Are the agents in here hooked up? Which one is it using?"

At the time `/api/agent/suggestions` was 500ing due to empty DB placeholders, not disconnected LLMs. But this question surfaced something else:

> "~/.claude/.env has three model groups you can use, add them all"

Three groups: `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY_1 + MODEL_1` (Opus), `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY_2 + MODEL_2` (Sonnet), `DASHSCOPE_API_KEY` (Qwen). The user's intent was direct: **these three can fallback to each other**.

Designing LLMPool had three core decisions:

**1. Provider Interface Design**

```typescript
interface LLMProvider {
  parseText(input: string, referenceDate?: string): Promise<ParsedEvent>
  parseImage?(imageBase64: string, mimeType: string): Promise<ParsedEvent>
  parseAudio?(audioBase64: string, mimeType: string): Promise<ParsedEvent>
}
```

`parseImage` and `parseAudio` are optional. Anthropic supports images, not audio; DashScope supports audio (via Qwen-Audio), not images (defaults to fallback). This means the pool needs to distinguish "method not supported (skip)" from "method failed (fallback)".

Solution is having callbacks return `undefined` for unsupported:

```typescript
await this.tryEach('parseImage', (p) =>
  p.parseImage ? p.parseImage(imageBase64, mimeType) : Promise.resolve(undefined)
)
```

In `tryEach`: `result === undefined` means this provider lacks this capability, continue to next; `throw` means attempt failed, also continue to next.

**2. Factory Injection vs Direct Construction**

Pool entries are `{ name: string; factory: () => LLMProvider }`, not holding provider instances directly. Reason is lazy construction on each call—avoids needless connections when you have 3 API keys. Tests inject fake providers directly, no need to mock global variables.

**3. Error Messages Must Include Provider Names**

When all providers fail, the thrown error must clearly state who failed and why:

```
[llm-pool] all providers failed for parseText:
  anthropic-1: timeout after 30s
  anthropic-2: rate limit exceeded
  dashscope: invalid API response
```

This isn't for aesthetics, it's for debugging. In production when the entire chain fails, you need to know if the first provider timed out or had auth failure.

---

## Two Truths About OPC Loop

This session used two rounds of OPC loop—first round ran complete app construction (previous session), second round specifically ran LLM Pool test coverage. The second round's plan was clear:

```
L1.1 review-existing → L1.2 implement-e2e → L1.3 review-e2e 
→ L1.4 implement-agent-test → L1.5 review-agent-test → L1.6 final-verify
```

**Truth 1: Review and implement must be separate**

L1.3's review found the core E2E testing problem: FabBar tests call real LLMs, meaning test stability depends on whether API keys work, network is stable, and LLMs feel like returning expected formats. This isn't a test, it's a prayer.

Fix was `page.route('**/api/llm/parse', ...)` interception, returning fixture ParsedEvent. Tests went from "depending on external services" to "verifying UI logic".

But this problem was caught by L1.3's review agent, not the L1.2 agent writing E2E. This is the value of OPC's "the agent that does the work never evaluates it"—the same agent looking at its own tests has no incentive to point out "this test shouldn't call real LLMs."

**Truth 2: Review quality depends on gate strictness**

L1.5's review of agent tests found two FAILs:

- T3's `toThrow('all providers failed')` too loose—if error message changes to "all LLM providers exhausted," substring match fails, but change to "all providers failed for WRONG_METHOD," substring match incorrectly passes
- T4's manual `try/finally` restore env more fragile than `vi.stubEnv`—will leak env state in concurrent test scenarios

Neither would "explode" immediately, but both would quietly become weird bugs eventually. Changing them to precise assertion + `vi.stubEnv` made test contracts clearer, maintenance cost lower.

Strict gate standards (🔴 = must fix, cannot continue) ensure reviews have actual impact. Mark T3's issue 🟡 "suggestion" and it likely gets forgotten in the next PR.

---

## Numbers

Final state of code generated in this session:

- **103 vitest tests** passing (9 test files, including unit, agent integration, E2E)
- **35 git commits**, from scaffold to LLM Pool tests
- **`src/lib/llm/pool.ts`**: 135 lines, implements three-way fallback, zero external dependencies
- **`tests/agent/llm-pool-agent.test.ts`**: 4 integration tests, covering success path, fallback chain, all-fail aggregated error, method-not-supported skip
- **tsc 0 errors**, no `any` in pool.ts

Session consumption: $92, 1389 minutes, 44 subagents. Cache_read tokens were 95% of total tokens (109M / 115M)—prompt caching is lifeline for long context sessions. Without cache, equivalent workload cost would be roughly 3-5x.

---

## What Didn't Go Well

**Agent Feed is currently stubbed**. AgentCard shows hardcoded suggestions like "suggest checking meeting conflicts," actual LLM agent integration isn't complete. `/api/agent/suggestions` is rule engine, not real LLM reasoning.

**`require()` used CommonJS in pool factory**. Factory functions in `buildPool()` use `require('./anthropic')`, which is technical debt in an ESM project. Should change to dynamic `import()`. Already in backlog, but didn't make this session.

**E2E has one path hitting real LLM** (`POST /api/llm/parse` smoke test). This isn't a bug, it's intentional integration smoke, but means CI environment needs valid API keys. This tradeoff is explicitly intentional, but worth documenting.

---

## On Using OPC Loop for Product Development

This session is a good reference point for understanding OPC loop boundaries in product development scenarios.

**Good for**: **Implementation tasks with clear specs** ("write these tests," "implement LLMPool"), **tasks needing review-implement cycles** (review finds issues → implement fixes → re-review confirms).

**Not good for**: **Iterations requiring real-time UI preview** ("adjust this button position" tasks need visual feedback loop can't provide), **steps requiring external account operations** (creating Vercel DB, configuring Google OAuth—these UI operations can't be automated).

Most important point: **OPC loop executes plans, doesn't generate them**. If direction is wrong (like making main interface a Calendar), loop will very efficiently go in the wrong direction. Direction must be clear before starting the loop.

Direction changes in this session happened outside the loop—user saw Calendar interface, said it was wrong, then re-brainstormed, confirmed Agent Feed direction before launching new loop. This sequence was correct.
